Q1: I tried to keep a pretty steady routine, honestly. I'd set aside about three hours a day, usually in the mornings before my part-time job or in the evenings when the house was quiet. Being an auditory learner, I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, which helped me fit studying into my day. I kept a basic weekly schedule written on paper because I couldn't always rely on having good internet access at home.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, but life doesn't always cooperate when you're working with limited resources. Deadlines definitely pushed me to adjust my schedule, and sometimes my extracurricular commitments meant I had to shuffle things around. But I tried to maintain at least some consistency because that's what keeps me sane with the stress levels I deal with.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was something specific I needed to check, like an assignment deadline or a new lecture being posted. Sometimes I'd get a notification reminder and that would prompt me. I also made it a habit to check in a few times a week just to make sure I wasn't missing anything important, even if I wasn't actively working on something.
Q4: Yes, after the first few weeks it became automatic to check the LMS in the morning with my coffee. I think what made it stick was connecting it to something I already did every day. The mindfulness practice I picked up also helped me be more intentional about my routines, so logging in became part of my daily rhythm rather than something I dreaded.
Q5: The recorded lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I'd often replay sections multiple times to really absorb the material. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives, even through text, gave me different angles on the content. I found that the combination of audio content and peer discussions worked really well for how my brain processes information.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't engage much with the reading materials or supplementary PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading long documents on my phone was frustrating. I also tended to skip optional video content that wasn't directly tied to assessments because I had to prioritize my time carefully given everything else on my plate.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to either an assignment or my understanding of core concepts. I also valued anything I could engage with through listening rather than reading. If something seemed like busywork or wasn't clearly explained in terms of its purpose, I'd probably deprioritize it because my time is genuinely limited.
Q8: Required activities always came first, no question. Optional ones felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford, even when I was interested. There was this constant mental calculation of whether the optional work would actually help my grade or understanding enough to justify the time investment. Sometimes I felt guilty about skipping them, but I had to be realistic about what I could manage.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings when I had good internet access and then listen to them repeatedly on my phone during my commute or while doing chores. I also borrowed textbooks from friends since I couldn't afford all the recommended materials. A lot of my actual learning happened through these offline methods.
Q10: There were many times I'd be actively studying but the LMS wouldn't know about it. I'd listen to downloaded lectures while walking to work, or I'd discuss course concepts with classmates in person during our extracurricular activities. Sometimes I'd spend hours going over my handwritten notes, which obviously doesn't show up anywhere online.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd open a lecture video once, download the audio if possible, and then that would be my study material for days. The LMS would only see that one access, but I might have listened to that same lecture five or six times over the following week. It was the most practical approach given my internet situation at home.
Q12: The middle of the semester was rough when multiple deadlines piled up and my work schedule got more demanding. I felt the stress building, and even though I wasn't anxious exactly, I was definitely overwhelmed. The mindfulness exercises helped me stay grounded during those times, but my engagement with the LMS definitely dipped when I was just trying to survive.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor for me. Working part-time while managing ten online courses meant I was often exhausted by the time I sat down to study. When I was tired, I'd just do the bare minimum on the LMS, maybe check deadlines or skim announcements, rather than actually engaging deeply with the content. Competing responsibilities definitely shaped when and how I used the platform.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I might have been worrying about bills or thinking about work. The LMS would register activity, but I wasn't really absorbing anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning.
Q15: I postponed some assignments even when I knew they mattered, usually because I was overwhelmed with other obligations. Sometimes the task felt too big to start when I was already stressed, so I'd put it off until the pressure became unavoidable. I'm not proud of my completion rate, but I had to make hard choices about where to put my limited energy.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver for LMS activity. I'd ramp up my logins and engagement significantly in the days before something was due. It wasn't ideal, but it was realistic for my situation. The deadline structure gave me clear priorities when everything else felt chaotic and competing for my attention.
Q17: When I got positive feedback, it definitely motivated me to engage more with similar activities. Knowing that my approach was working made me feel like my efforts were worthwhile despite all the challenges. Constructive criticism was helpful too, though sometimes it was hard to act on it immediately when I was already stretched thin.
Q18: The course structure with clear weekly modules helped me know what to expect and when. I appreciated having predictable patterns because it let me plan around my work schedule and other commitments. When the structure was clear, I could be strategic about my engagement even if I couldn't always be consistent.
Q19: Having audio content available was crucial for me as an auditory learner, so courses that relied heavily on that format were much easier to engage with. What made things harder was when important information was buried in long text documents or when the navigation was confusing. Simple, clear organization made a huge difference in whether I could keep up.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is searching for something specific or trying to understand the system but struggling with the actual work. Maybe they're confused about requirements or dealing with personal issues that prevent them from completing assignments. High activity doesn't necessarily mean productive activity, it might just mean someone is lost or anxious.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like my situation. I performed reasonably well despite not being super active on the LMS because so much of my learning happened offline. I'd download materials, study independently, discuss with peers in person, and only submit the final work. The grade reflected my learning, not my platform activity.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone logged in and what they clicked on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone was genuinely focused or just had the page open while doing something else. They also miss all the offline learning, the conversations with classmates, the repeated listening to downloaded lectures, the handwritten notes.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I find myself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when I want to discuss ideas with others. It's a mental investment that goes beyond just showing up and checking boxes. Even when my LMS activity was low, I felt engaged when the content mattered to me.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the mindfulness intervention actually helped me manage my stress and stay focused despite everything. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures while commuting or the study groups I formed with classmates outside the platform. It also wouldn't show how happy and accomplished I felt by the end, despite all the challenges I faced with limited resources.
